title: Career Day: Twelve Jobs, Zero Decisions Made

targetWords.zh: 推銷員/推銷員/女推銷員

sentences.en: Monday. School held Career Day today, where different adults from the neighborhood came in to talk about their jobs.
sentences.zh: 星期一。今天學校辦了職業日，附近社區不同的大人來學校講他們的工作。

sentences.en: The idea is that we are supposed to leave with some idea of what we want to be when we grow up.
sentences.zh: 這個活動的用意是，我們離開的時候應該對長大想做什麼多少有點想法。

sentences.en: I left with twelve completely different, equally confusing ideas, which I am told is a very normal outcome for a thirteen-year-old.
sentences.zh: 我離開的時候有十二個完全不同、同樣讓人困惑的想法，聽說對一個十三歲的孩子來說，這是很正常的結果。

sentences.en: The first booth belonged to a local fisherman, who told us stories about waking up before sunrise to check his nets.
sentences.zh: 第一個攤位是一位當地漁夫，他跟我們講了他天還沒亮就起床檢查漁網的故事。

sentences.en: "Being a fisherman is not romantic every day," he admitted. "Some mornings the ocean gives you nothing at all."
sentences.zh: 「當漁夫並不是每天都浪漫，」他坦承：「有些早晨，大海什麼都不給你。」

sentences.en: Next was a sailor who had traveled to more countries than I could name, describing life on a huge cargo ship.
sentences.zh: 接下來是一位水手，他去過的國家比我能講出來的還多，他描述了在一艘巨大貨輪上的生活。

sentences.en: Bernice asked if being a sailor gets lonely, and he said yes, but the ocean at night makes up for a lot of that loneliness.
sentences.zh: Bernice 問當水手會不會孤單，他說會，但夜晚的大海會彌補很多那種孤單感。

sentences.en: A local gardener showed us photos of a public park she had shaped over twenty years, one flower bed at a time.
sentences.zh: 一位當地的園丁給我們看她花了二十年、一床花壇一床花壇整理出來的公園照片。

sentences.en: "People walk through my work every day and never know my name," she said. "That is fine. The garden remembers."
sentences.zh: 「人們每天走過我的作品，卻從來不知道我的名字，」她說：「這樣也好，花園記得就夠了。」

sentences.en: A friendly barber demonstrated how he cuts hair while somehow also managing a shop full of chatting customers at the same time.
sentences.zh: 一位親切的理髮師示範他怎麼一邊剪頭髮，一邊還能同時應付一整間客人聊個不停的店。

sentences.en: A shopkeeper who runs the little store near our school explained how she orders products, manages a small budget, and still finds time to remember every regular customer's favorite snack.
sentences.zh: 在我們學校附近開小店的一位店主，解釋她怎麼進貨、管理有限的預算，還能記得每位常客最愛的零食。

sentences.en: A businessman in a very sharp suit talked about managing a whole company, being both an employer and, once, a very nervous employee himself.
sentences.zh: 一位穿著非常筆挺西裝的商人，講述他管理整間公司的經歷，他既是雇主，也曾經是一位非常緊張的員工。

sentences.en: "A good manager remembers what it felt like to be a new employee," he said. "I try not to forget that feeling."
sentences.zh: 「一位好的主管會記得自己剛當員工時的感覺，」他說：「我試著不去忘記那種感覺。」

sentences.en: A salesperson explained that the hardest part of her job is not selling things, it is listening carefully to what people actually need.
sentences.zh: 一位銷售員解釋，她工作最難的部分不是賣東西，而是仔細聽出人們真正需要的是什麼。

sentences.en: A waiter from a busy restaurant downtown talked about carrying six plates at once without ever mixing up a single order.
sentences.zh: 市區一間忙碌餐廳的服務生，講述他怎麼一次端六個盤子，卻從來不會搞錯任何一份訂單。

sentences.en: "Being a good host matters just as much as good food," he said. "People remember how you made them feel."
sentences.zh: 「當一位好的接待者，跟食物好吃一樣重要，」他說：「人們會記得你讓他們有什麼感覺。」

sentences.en: A police officer talked about long night shifts and how much of the job is actually just being a calm, steady presence for people in trouble.
sentences.zh: 一位警官談到漫長的夜班，以及這份工作有多少時候其實只是在人們遇到麻煩時，當一個冷靜、穩定的存在。

sentences.en: A local author read us a short page from her newest book and admitted that most days, writing feels less magical and more like stubborn effort.
sentences.zh: 一位當地作家唸了她最新一本書的短短一頁，她坦承大部分日子裡，寫作感覺沒那麼神奇，比較像是頑固的努力。

sentences.en: A film director showed a short clip and explained how directing a movie is mostly about solving one small problem after another, all day long.
sentences.zh: 一位電影導演播了一小段片段，解釋執導一部電影，大部分時候其實是一整天不斷解決一個接一個的小問題。

sentences.en: A professional musician and a dancer performed together for five minutes, and honestly, that booth got the loudest applause of the entire day.
sentences.zh: 一位職業音樂家和一位舞者一起表演了五分鐘，老實說，那個攤位得到了今天全場最大的掌聲。

sentences.en: Even a retired radio operator came, describing a career spent connecting calls long before smartphones existed.
sentences.zh: 甚至還有一位退休的電話總機接線員也來了，講述在智慧型手機出現以前，他靠接通電話維生的職業生涯。

sentences.en: One booth belonged to a former professional goalkeeper, who said the loneliest position in sports taught him more patience than any other job could.
sentences.zh: 有一個攤位是一位前職業守門員，他說運動場上最孤單的位置，教會他的耐心比其他任何工作都還多。

sentences.en: By the end of the day, Bernice had decided she wants to be a sailor, a musician, and a shopkeeper, all at the same time.
sentences.zh: 到了一天結束，Bernice 已經決定她想同時當水手、音樂家，還有店主。

sentences.en: I still have no idea what I want to be, but I know I want a job where, like the gardener said, the work remembers me even if the people do not.
sentences.zh: 我還是不知道自己想做什麼，但我知道，我想要一份工作，就像那位園丁說的，就算人們不記得我，工作本身還是記得我。

quiz.question: What does the gardener say people rarely know about her work?
options: How long she has worked there.

quiz.question: According to the salesperson, what is the hardest part of her job?
options: Listening carefully to what people actually need.

quiz.question: What does the former goalkeeper say his position taught him?
options: More patience than any other job could.
How to run faster than anyone else.
How to manage a whole team.
How to cook for a large crowd.
